title: Lesson 368
transcript: So we've been talking about digital forensics here and the forensic investigation process. And there's one little piece that we have to add in here, which is we have to talk in a little more detail about some specific types of evidence. Because there's four of these, especially four of these, that you might get questions about on the exam. We're gonna walk you through all the different types of evidence, but there's four in particular that we're gonna focus on. The first important one that you need to know about is what's called real evidence. And what real evidence means is that it's a tangible, physical object, so like the literal smoking gun at the crime scene, that would be real evidence because it's a tangible object. Now, really importantly, and this is important to remember for the exam, data on anything, on a hard drive, on an SSD, on a CD, whatever, is not considered to be real evidence. Because as human beings, you look at a hard drive, you can't see the data. You can't see the ones and zeros. And even if you could see the ones and zeros, we can't translate that in our minds into a picture or a video There's no direct proof of its veracity, right? That's the word that they use. It's degree of truth. Yeah, exactly. Yeah. So just remember, real evidence means it's a tangible physical object, which data is not. Yeah. The next one that you see there is direct evidence, keyword direct, which means it speaks for itself and there's no inference element associated with it. It's direct evidence that obviously is provided through You know, confessions, for example, also that smoking gun and so on. But it's direct evidence that obviously, you know, hopefully would be very valuable as far as investigations are concerned. Yeah, it requires no interpretation, no inference, right? The smoking gun with the killer's fingerprint on it and the ballistics match the victim. Excellent. Yeah, no inference required. Now, the next two, next three, actually, circumstantial, collaborative, and hearsay evidence, these are, you know, these are obviously exist from a legal perspective, but you're very unlikely to get questions on them. So we've included them here for completeness, but we're not going to go through them in And if you watch crime shows, you hear those words all the time. Hearsay evidence is not direct evidence that can be admissible, for example. Someone heard someone saying something else. Very reliable. Yeah, that's right. Next one is best evidence. And best evidence, again, keyword best here. I mean, original evidence. It's not a copy. There's no secondary element to it, right? It's the original, best evidence. Yeah, imagine you're in a courtroom and you're presenting your evidence. What would the court prefer, the original or a photocopy? Exactly. Yep. That's the best evidence rule. And then the final one, this ties back to the very first one we talked about, right? Real evidence. Secondary evidence is a reproduction or a substitute and data is considered to be secondary evidence because we have to reproduce it with an algorithm or something like that for us to be able to understand it and so remember that for the exam secondary evidence is data is considered to be secondary evidence yeah and it might be worthwhile just reiterating here I know we've done it before but what you usually see in yellow on our slides is what's really important because it certainly addresses what people are seeing on the exam and what's in the exam outline, which obviously is important. Yeah, there's so much you have to memorize, we try and make it easy for you. So there you go, types of evidence. Excellent.
